pages.text: Study on the graphite and carbon ﬁber modiﬁed asphalt concrete Liu Xiaoming a,⇑, Wu Shaopeng b a School of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Central South University, Changsha 410075, China b Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials Science and Engineering of Ministry of Education, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan 430070, China article info Article history: Received 19 May 2010 Received in revised form 11 October 2010 Accepted 13 November 2010 Available online 9 December 2010 Keywords: Asphalt concrete Indirect tensile test Mechanical property Electrical property Self-monitoring abstract The mechanical and electrical properties of graphite and carbon ﬁber modiﬁed asphalt concrete were measured in this paper through the indirect tensile test. The experimental results indicate that the mechanical properties of asphalt mixture are inﬂuenced by the addition of conductive component like graphite and carbon ﬁber, When the graphite content increased from 0 to 22 vol.%, Marshall stability decreased from 12.8 kN to 9.43 kN and residual stability from 91.1% to 87.2%. Rutting dynamic stability decreased from 3318 times/mm to 2619 times/mm. After adding the carbon ﬁber, when the carbon ﬁber content increased from 0 to 2 vol.%, their Marshall stability and residual stability increased from 12.8 kN to 13.5 kN and residual stability from 91.1% to 92.7%. Rutting dynamic stability increased from 3318 times/mm to 3403 times/mm. When modiﬁed through combination effect of graphite and carbon ﬁber mixed ﬁllers, the mechanical performance and electrical property were improved greatly, their Marshall stability and residual stability has increased from 9.43 kN to 12.1 kN and residual stability from 87.2% to 89.2%. Rutting dynamic stability increased from 2619 times/mm to 3292 times/mm. Further- more, the resilient modulus is inﬂuenced by the addition of graphite, the value of resilient modulus is as much as that of the original, it is 90% when graphite is 30 vol.% and is 70% when graphite is 45 vol.%. The electrical resistance increases reversibly with increasing tensile strain either under dynamic indirect tensile testing or static indirect tensile testing, graphite and carbon ﬁber modiﬁed asphalt con- cretes are effective for the self-monitoring of strain. It is foreseen that the strain–stress self monitoring is valuable for weighing, trafﬁc monitoring, border monitoring and structural vibration control. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Asphalt concrete is widely used for pavements, bridge deck, air- ports road and related structural applications. However, the simul- taneous use of these materials for structural functions is desired for the purpose of providing smart structures and alleviating the high cost, low durability and design complexity associated with using separate structural components in the same structure. It is theoretically feasible to improve the conductivity of asphalt concrete by the addition of conductive ﬁllers or additives to melt and remove snow and ice on asphalt pavement thermo- electrically. Furthermore, the self-monitoring application of con- ductive asphalt concrete was reported [1]. This is expected to become one of the most efﬁcient, convenient, safe and environ- mental conservative methods to remove snow and ice [2]. The addition of conductive powders into an insulating polymer matrix is traditionally an ideal method to improve the conductivity of the composites because of well dispersion. Accordin

stress has been reported in a composite containing graphite parti- cles [8]; the resistance increased with increasing compressive stress in a partly reversible fashion. The multitude of tests available for investigating the crack development (e.g. bending test, indirect tensile test (ITT), direct tensile test) reveals the necessity to select a relatively simple, cost-effective, fast and practicable test method which features an approximately accurate simulation of the real stress ratios in a pavement. The ITT is such a test method. Cores from existing pave- ments as well as specimens prepared in the laboratory can be tested without having to implement new procedures for the spec- imen setting-up. In the case of the ITT, the resultant deformations are measured for a cylindrical specimen which is loaded by two diametrically arranged compressive forces applied via curved load distribution platens. The dynamic ITT implies cyclical loading of the specimen up to a deﬁned criteria for test termination (fatigue crack, marginal number of load cycles). The paper studies the engineering properties and electrical property of asphalt concrete with different graphite and CF con- tent. Due to its advantage for revealing the fatigue property of as- phalt concrete, this paper addresses graphite and CF modiﬁed asphalt concretes for self-monitoring applications and its mechan- ical properties by ITT. 2. Experimental methods 2.1. Raw materials The materials and their properties are shown in Table 1 . In this study, the Superpave 12.5 composition was prepared for electrical resistivity measurement and indirect tensile test specimens shown in Table 2, The aggregates were a mixture of 23% crushed stone (4.75–9.5 mm), 72% ﬁne crushed stone (<2.36 mm) and 5% ﬁll- ers (<0.075 mm) by weight, which was 4.2–5.2% for asphalt. Three specimens of each composition were prepared by gyrate compactor (EP-31111 model, America) at the temperature of 155 ± 1 /C176C. Gyrate numbers are N ini =9 , Ndes = 125, and Nmax = 205 [9]. The size of the specimen is 150 ± 5 mm in diameter, and 130 ± 5 mm in height. 2.2. Tests methods 2.2.1. ITT test [10] Testing was performed at 15 /C176C under cyclic indirect tensile testing shown in Fig 1 . A hydraulic mechanical testing system (UTM-25, Australia) was used, until the specimens were destructed totally. During mechanical testing, the longitudinal strain was measured by using a strain gauge. Three specimens of each composition were tested to ascertain reproducibility of the trends reported here. 2.2.2. ITT resilient modulus testing During operation of the test, a pulsed diametral loading force is applied to the vertical axis of a specimen and the resulting total recoverable diametral deforma- tion in the horizontal axis is then measured. The loading pulse width equals to 200 ms, repetition period equals to 3000 ms, peak load equals to 1500 N and wave shape is haversine. The test sequence consists of 15 conditioning loading pulses fol- lowed by ﬁve test loading pulses from which the ﬁnal data are calculated. The test temperature is 15 /C176C. Esp ¼ PT ð0:27 þ lÞ XT h ð1Þ where Esp is the resilient modulus, MPa; PT the repeated loading, N; h the diameter of specimen, mm; XT the recoverable horizontal deformation, mm; l is the Poisson’s ra- tio in test temperature, l = 0.5 in the paper. 2.2.3. Electrical measurements Simultaneously with mechanical testing, the electrical resistance measure- ments were made by a Keithley 2700 multimeter (USA

Furthermore, the variety tendency of output resistance is con- sistent with that of strain, so the variation of strain is reﬂected by the variation of resistance. It has good consistency between the variation of output resistance and the variation of strain either the initial resistance is higher than 10 3 or lower than 10 2. Fig. 5 shows that the longitudinal resistance change behavior during dynamic indirect tensile test up to failure totally. Wu / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 1807–1811

resistance change can be divided into three phases: The resistance deceasing with the specimen was more compressed in the ﬁrst phase, and then it changed little in the second phase because the specimen was in steady state after compressed. Finally the resis- tance increasing with the specimen was destructed gradually. The increase or decrease of resistance can be thought to be got by the variation result of internal structure of asphalt concrete. Compression action can form more conductive paths and the resis- tance decrease. In the meanwhile, material failure can cut conduc- tive path and the resistance increase. 3.3.1. Self monitoring under dynamic ITT Fig. 6 shows that the fractional change in resistance (thick curve) versus time and of longitudinal displacement (thin curve) versus time at 0.05 mm/min static tensile testing up to failure. Firstly the output resistance decrease slightly, and then starts to in- crease at 60% fatigue lifetime. Finally it increased abruptly with the increase of longitudinal displacement at 90% fatigue lifetime. Fig. 7 shows similar behavior of asphalt concrete with different graphite content under the same loading model. According to above results, as for graphite and carbon ﬁber mixed modiﬁed asphalt concretes, the fractional change in resis- tance corresponds with the deformation of asphalt concrete either under dynamic indirect tensile test or static indirect tensile testing when its resistance is lower than 10 3 or less. Therefore, it can mon- itor its internal deformation and defects by measurement of resis- tance change. This strain-monitoring is valuable for weighing, trafﬁc monitoring, border monitoring, and structural vibration control. 4. Conclusions The experimental results indicate that the mechanical proper- ties of asphalt mixture is inﬂuenced by the addition of conductive component like graphite and carbon ﬁber, When the graphite con- tent increased from 0 to 22 vol.%, Marshall stability decreased from 12.8 kN to 9.43 kN and residual stability from 91.1% to 87.2%. Rutting dynamic stability decreased from 3318 times/mm to 2619 times/mm. After adding the carbon ﬁber, when the CF content increased from 0 to 2 vol.%, their Marshall stability and residual stability increased from 12.8 kN to 13.5 kN and residual stability from 91.1% to 92.7%. Rutting dynamic stability decreased from 3318 times/mm to 3403 times/mm. When modiﬁed through combination effect of graphite and CF mixed ﬁllers, their Marshall stability and residual stability has increased from 9.43 kN to 12.1 kN and residual stability from 87.2% to 89.2%. Rutting dy- namic stability decreased from 2619 times/mm to 3292 times/ mm. Furthermore, the resilient modulus is inﬂuenced by the addi- tion of graphite, the value of resilient modulus is as much as that of the original, it is 90% when graphite is 30 vol.% and is 70% when graphite is 45 vol.%. The electrical resistance increases reversibly with increasing tensile strain either under dynamic indirect tensile testing or static indirect tensile testing. Graphite and carbon ﬁber modiﬁed asphalt concretes are effective for the self-monitoring of strain. References [1] Liu XM, Wu SP, Li Ning, et al. Self-monitoring application of asphalt concrete containing graphite ans carbon ﬁbers. J Wuhan Univ Of Tech (Mater Sci Ed) 2008;23(2):268–71. [2] Liu XM, Wu SP, Li Ning, et al. Smart characteristic of conductive asphalt concrete. J Cent South Univ (Sci Tech) 2009;40(5):1465–70 (in chinese). [3] We

3. Results and discussion 3.1. Mechanical properties 3.1.1. Engineering properties From Table 3, due to graphite powder absorbing oil, we can ﬁnd that the optimal asphalt content increase from 4.2 to 5.2 when the graphite content increased from 0 to 22 vol.%, their Marshall stabil- ity and residual stability has decreased correspondingly, Marshall stability decreased from 12.8 down to 9.43 and residual stability from 91.1 to 87.2. Rutting dynamic stability decreased from 3318 to 2619. The addition of graphite does not enhance the mechanical strength to conductive composites because of its intrinsic feature. It strongly inﬂuences the mechanical strength as ﬁller content is beyond a certain level. The reason is probably that graphite crystal- lizes in the hexagonal system and has a sheet like structure where the atoms all lie in a plane and are only weakly bonded to the graphite sheets above and below, so graphite is a very good lubri- cant. It has been demonstrated that some properties including con- ductive property and mechanical property change dramatically near percolation threshold in two-phase composites. The forma- tion of graphite-particle network improves conductive property remarkably, otherwise, impairs the mechanical properties observ- ably. Therefore, the graphite content should be controlled to certain low content in order to prepare asphalt concrete with both excellent conductive property and mechanical property. After adding the carbon ﬁber, from Table 3 we can ﬁnd that the optimal asphalt content increase from 4.2 to 4.3 when the CF con- tent increased from 0 to 2 vol.%, their Marshall stability and resid- ual stability increased from 12.8 to 13.5 and residual stability from 91.1 to 92.7. Rutting dynamic stability decreased from 3318 to 3403. When modiﬁed through combination effect of graphite and CF mixed ﬁllers, the mechanical performance and electrical pros- perity were improved greatly, their Marshall stability and residual stability has increased from 9.43 to 12.1 and residual stability from 87.2 to 89.2. Rutting dynamic stability decreased from 2619 to 3292. On one hand, CF has a conductive bridging effect, which is deﬁned that ﬁbers may bridge up several isolated conductive clus- ters and stride over the hindrance on conductive chains created by some ﬁne aggregates. It also may astride large aggregates to connect conductive clusters or chains together and increase the probability for forming a conductive network. On the other hand, a shortcut effect makes it shorter, easier and more efﬁcient to con- duct electricity for such a crooked and knaggy chain-type of con- ductive paths in asphalt concrete. Conductive particles exhibit short-range contacts, further enhancing the connection of the adja- cent ﬁbers. Therefore, the conductive performance is remarkably improved through combination effect of mixed ﬁllers. 3.1.2. Resilient modulus Fig 2 shows that the resilient modulus variation of asphalt con- cretes with different graphite powders (0%, 10%, 30%, 45% by vol- ume of asphalt) and different carbon ﬁbers (A: 0%, B: 1%, C: 2% by volume of asphalt). These data show that the mechanical perfor- mance was inﬂuenced by the addition of graphite powder and that resilient modulus decrease. When it has only 10 vol.% graphite, the value of resilient modulus is as much as that of the original. How- ever the resilient modulus decreases with the increase of graphite content, it is 90% that of the original when graphite is 30 vol
